title: [FIRRTL] Parse CircuitTarget and ModuleTarget Annotations
body:
Adds the ability to parse and store CircuitTarget or ModuleTarget
local Annotations as MLIR Attributes.  Annotations are stringly-typed
in an array of attributes.  The "target" field is removed
as part of this process as this is now implicit based on the operation
to which the Attribute is applied.
    
Any other types of Annotations (e.g., ComponentTarget or non-local)
produce a runtime error with JSON diagnostic information printing.
    
The utility firtool is updated to take a --annotation-file argument
which can be used to import annotations from an annotation file.
Additionally, a non-standard inline annotation syntax is added.

Annotations ingested via --annotation-file are appended to existing inline annotations. 

### Example 1: Input Annotation File

Given the following circuit:

```scala
circuit Foo:
  module Bar:
    input a: UInt<1>
    output b: UInt<1>

    b <= a
  module Foo:
    input a: UInt<1>
    output b: UInt<1>

    inst bar of Bar
    bar.a <= a
    b <= bar.b
```

And the following annotation file:
```json
[
  {
    "class":"firrtl.passes.InlineAnnotation",
    "target":"Foo.Bar"
  },
  {
    "class":"firrtl.transforms.NoDedupAnnotation",
    "target":"~Foo|Bar"
  },
  {
    "class":"firrtl.stage.RunFirrtlTransformAnnotation",
    "transform":"firrtl.passes.InlineInstances"
  }
]
```

You can import this into CIRCT using:

```bash
firtool Foo.fir --annotation-file Foo.anno.json
```

To get a bunch of `#firrtl.*` attributes:

```mlir
module  {
  firrtl.circuit "Foo" attributes {annotations = [{class = "firrtl.stage.RunFirrtlTransformAnnotation", transform = "firrtl.passes.InlineInstances"}]} {
    firrtl.module @Bar(%a: !firrtl.uint<1>, %b: !firrtl.flip<uint<1>>) attributes {annotations = [{class = "firrtl.passes.InlineAnnotation"}, {class = "firrtl.transforms.NoDedupAnnotation"}]} {
      firrtl.connect %b, %a : !firrtl.flip<uint<1>>, !firrtl.uint<1>
    }
    firrtl.module @Foo(%a: !firrtl.uint<1>, %b: !firrtl.flip<uint<1>>) {
      %bar_a, %bar_b = firrtl.instance @Bar {name = "bar", portNames = ["a", "b"]} : !firrtl.flip<uint<1>>, !firrtl.uint<1>
      firrtl.connect %bar_a, %a : !firrtl.flip<uint<1>>, !firrtl.uint<1>
      firrtl.connect %b, %bar_b : !firrtl.flip<uint<1>>, !firrtl.uint<1>
    }
  }
}
```

The above can be round tripped into/out of an `*.mlir` file.

### Example 2: Inline Annotation Syntax

The above can be represented using a non-standard _inline annotation syntax_. Here, some JSON is packed into a `%[...]` area after the circuit. I'm working with the other Chisel/FIRRTL developers to see about getting something like this supported by the Scala FIRRTL Compiler and into the spec. I expect that this will change in the future and we can add support for the new syntax once consensus is reached.

Example:

```scala
circuit Bar: %[[{"class":"Foo"},{"class":"Bar","target":"~Bar|Bar"}]] @[Bar.scala 42:42]
  module Bar: @[Bar.scala 43:43]
    skip
```

This then parses into:

```mlir
module  {
  firrtl.circuit "Bar" attributes {annotations = [{class = "Foo"}]} {
    firrtl.module @Bar() attributes {annotations = [{class = "Bar"}]} {
    }
  }
}
```

### Example 3: Invalid JSON

If there is actual invalid JSON, this should be reported correctly. Due to the fact that `llvm::json::ParseError` does not have public line/column/offset members, the error can't be great. (If this was public, then we should be able to integrate this with a good source manager location error.)

Consider the following which includes invalid JSON (I used a `,` instead of a `:`):

```scala
circuit Foo: %[[{"a","a"}]]
  module Foo:
    skip
```

This will error like so:

```
annotations/ParserFailure.fir:1:14: error: Failed to parse inline JSON annotations
circuit Foo: %[[{"a","a"}]]
             ^
annotations/ParserFailure.fir:1:14: note: [1:6, byte=6]: Expected : after object key
```

### Example 4: Invalid/Unsupported Annotation JSON

This PR only adds support for annotations without a target (some subclass of [`firrtl.annotations.NoTargetAnnotation`](https://www.chisel-lang.org/api/firrtl/1.4.2/firrtl/annotations/NoTargetAnnotation.html)) or annotations targeting the entire circuit or a specific module. This PR treats anything else as an error.

This strict requirement can be lessened by, in the future, increasing support or by adding an option that promotes other annotations to target the entire circuit or drops them. I wanted to start with the strict option and expand functionality from there.

Additionally, annotations need to be an array of objects. Anything else is invalid.

Consider the following which includes a reference target, `~Baz|Baz>a`:

```scala
circuit Baz: %[[{"hello":"world","target":"~Baz|Baz>a"}]]
  module Baz:
    input a: UInt<1>
    output b: UInt<1>

    b <= a
```

When compiled this produces the following error:

```
annotations/ReferenceTarget.fir:1:14: error: Invalid/unsupported annotation format
circuit Baz: %[[{"hello":"world","target":"~Baz|Baz>a"}]]
             ^
annotations/ReferenceTarget.fir:1:14: note: See inline comments for problem area in JSON:
[
  {
    "hello": "world",
    "target": /* error: Unsupported target (not a CircuitTarget or ModuleTarget) */ "~Baz|Baz>a"
  }
]
```

### TODO

- [x] Figure out how to represent annotations inline in a `*.fir` file
- [x] Add tests
- [x] Use better error message reporting (emit an error on the circuit op and/or investigate the ability of `llvm::json::Path` to point to a specific problematic Annotation in the JSON
- [x] Switch to stringly-type `DictionaryAttr` to store arbitrary annotations
